items.prompt: A target-sum solver uses only positive candidate values. When is pruning by remainingTarget < 0 sound?

options.text: When every future choice is positive, so the remaining target can only decrease further.
For arbitrary positive and negative candidates.
Only because the candidates are sorted.
Only after a complete path is built.

feedback.reason: Sound pruning requires a proof that no future choice can repair the violated condition.
feedback.details: With positive candidates, overshooting the target is irreversible within that branch.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.any_numbers: A later negative or compensating value could restore feasibility.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_sorted: Sorting alone does not establish monotonic sum behavior.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_at_leaf: The point of pruning is to reject impossible partial states early.

items.prompt: Why is the same remainingTarget < 0 prune unsafe when negative candidates are allowed?

options.text: A later negative value can increase the remaining target back toward zero.
Negative values cannot be compared.
Recursion cannot process negative values.
Negative values prevent sorting.

feedback.reason: The monotonicity assumption behind the prune no longer holds.
feedback.details: A partial overshoot is not proof of impossibility when later choices can offset it.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.negative_values_uncomparable: They can; the issue is reversibility of the sum condition.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.recursion_forbidden: Recursion is not restricted by sign.

items.prompt: A k-combination solver has chosen c values and has r candidate positions left. Which condition proves completion is impossible?

options.text: r < k - c.
r = k - c.
c < k.

feedback.reason: A branch cannot fill more required slots than the number of candidates still available.
feedback.details: This capacity bound is independent of candidate values and is safe for combination enumeration.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.r_equals_needed: That means exactly enough candidates remain and may force a valid continuation.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.c_less_k: Most nonterminal states satisfy this and still can complete.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.r_positive: Having candidates remaining does not prove impossibility.

items.prompt: A partial assignment already violates a constraint that every full solution must satisfy. What is the correct action?

options.text: Prune the branch immediately.
Continue until every variable is assigned.
Record the partial assignment as a result.
Remove all sibling branches as well.

feedback.reason: A hereditary constraint violation makes every descendant invalid.
feedback.details: The proof must rely on the constraint semantics, not merely on the branch appearing inconvenient.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.continue_all_depths: No completion can remove an already mandatory violation unless the state model says it is reversible.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.record_partial: It is neither complete nor valid.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.delete_siblings: The violation is local to the current branch.

items.prompt: A palindrome-partition solver considers a segment that is not a palindrome. Why can that candidate be skipped?

options.text: Every result segment must be a palindrome, and later boundaries cannot change the chosen segment.
Long segments are always invalid.
The branch would probably be slow.
Every repeated segment is invalid.

feedback.reason: Once a boundary fixes the next segment, its validity is permanent within that branch.
feedback.details: An invalid segment cannot become valid by partitioning the remaining suffix.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.branch_slow: Expected cost is not a correctness proof.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicate_segment: Repetition is not the relevant contract.

items.prompt: Which statement describes branch ordering rather than pruning?

options.text: Try candidates likely to succeed before other still-legal candidates.
Do not recurse into a state proven unable to complete.
Reject a segment that violates the mandatory predicate.
Stop when too few candidates remain to fill required slots.

feedback.reason: Ordering changes traversal priority but retains every legal branch unless the result contract short-circuits after success.
feedback.details: Pruning removes a branch and therefore requires a sound impossibility argument.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.skip_invalid_segment: This is sound pruning or legality filtering.

items.prompt: A developer prunes a branch because its current score is worse than the best complete score, but future choices may improve the score. What is missing?

options.text: A proven bound showing that no completion of the branch can beat the best result.
A deeper recursion limit.
A sorted candidate list.
A set of previously returned outputs.

feedback.reason: Optimization pruning needs a lower or upper bound on the best possible completion of the partial state.
feedback.details: Comparing only the current partial score may discard a branch that later becomes optimal.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.more_recursion: Depth does not justify the prune.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting: Sorting alone provides no optimization bound.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicate_set: Output deduplication does not prove optimality.

items.prompt: A grid path still needs L characters but fewer than L unused cells remain reachable under a proven bound. What does this establish?

options.text: The branch cannot complete the target and may be pruned.
The remaining path is guaranteed to exist.
No starting position can ever succeed.
Visited state no longer needs restoration.

feedback.reason: A completion requiring more resources than the branch can possibly supply is impossible.
feedback.details: The reachability bound must itself be correct; an underestimate could prune valid paths.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.guaranteed_success: Insufficient capacity proves failure, not success.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.global_failure: The bound applies to the current branch.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.undo_optional: Restoration is still needed before returning to the parent.

items.prompt: Which are sound pruning reasons? Select all that apply.

options.text: A mandatory constraint is already irreversibly violated.
Too few candidates remain to fill required slots.
A positive-only sum branch has already exceeded the target.
The branch looks less promising than its siblings without a proof.

feedback.reason: Sound pruning follows from a proven impossibility condition.
feedback.details: Heuristic appearance may guide ordering but cannot justify omission.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.positive_overshoot: Future choices can only increase the sum.

items.prompt: Which proposed pruning rules are unsafe without additional assumptions? Select all that apply.

options.text: Prune target-sum states whenever the current sum exceeds target, even though negative values remain.
Prune an optimization branch because its current partial score is worse, without a completion bound.
Prune every partition segment longer than three when no length constraint exists.
Prune a branch whose prefix already violates a hereditary validity constraint.

feedback.reason: A pruning rule must be tied to explicit monotonicity, capacity, or hereditary constraints.
feedback.details: The invalid-prefix rule is safe when the stated constraint cannot be repaired by extension.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.invalid_prefix: That is sound because the violation persists.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.overshoot_with_negatives: Negative values may repair the overshoot.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.worse_partial_score: Future decisions may improve the score.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.long_segment: The rule is invented rather than contract-derived.

items.prompt: Which facts can support a sound remaining-capacity bound? Select all that apply.

options.text: The number of choices still required.
The number of legal candidates still available.
Whether candidates may be reused.
How many recursive calls have already occurred.

feedback.reason: Capacity pruning compares required future work with the maximum legal supply.
feedback.details: The bound must reflect reuse and candidate constraints accurately.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.global_call_count: Instrumentation does not determine branch feasibility.

items.prompt: Which grid conditions can justify pruning the current path? Select all that apply.

options.text: The current cell does not match the required target symbol.
The current cell is already used by the path when reuse is forbidden.
The coordinates are outside the grid.
The preferred neighbor is not tried first.

feedback.reason: These checks reject states that violate immediate legality.
feedback.details: Neighbor ordering can affect performance but not correctness.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.neighbor_order: Traversal order does not make the branch impossible.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.current_mismatch: This branch cannot represent the target prefix.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.cell_reused: The move violates the path contract.

items.prompt: Arrange the process for designing a pruning rule.

elements.text: State the partial condition to be tested.
Prove that every completion from that condition violates the result contract.
Check the condition before expanding the branch.
Test cases near the boundary where the condition becomes true or false.

feedback.reason: Pruning begins with a semantic proof, not with code or performance intuition.
feedback.details: Boundary tests help detect a condition that is too strong or too weak.

items.prompt: Arrange the remaining-slot capacity check for k-combination generation.

elements.text: Compute how many candidate indices remain.
If available < needed, return from the branch.

feedback.reason: The branch is expanded only when its remaining candidate capacity can satisfy the completion size.
feedback.details: This bound preserves completeness and can eliminate large impossible suffixes.

items.prompt: A backtracking tree has branching factor b and depth d. Sound pruning removes many branches on typical inputs but no reduction is guaranteed in the worst case. Select the worst-case visited-node bound and recursion depth.
constraints: Some inputs may satisfy no pruning condition until the leaves.

feedback.reason: Pruning may improve actual exploration without changing the formal worst-case class.
feedback.details: Worst-case analysis must include inputs on which every branch remains feasible until deep in the tree.

items.prompt: A subset search makes an include-or-exclude choice for each of n values. Positive-only target pruning may reduce actual work, but select the standard worst-case leaves and recursion depth.
constraints: A branch can make two choices at each input position.
No pruning reduction is guaranteed for every input.

feedback.reason: The binary decision tree has up to 2^n leaves, while one active path contains n decisions.
feedback.details: Pruning can make many practical instances smaller but does not erase the worst-case tree.

items.prompt: An n-variable assignment solver tries unused values and uses conflict pruning. If every partial assignment remains conflict-free until full depth, select the number of complete branches and recursion depth.
constraints: Each of n distinct values is used exactly once.

feedback.reason: When pruning finds no conflict, the solver still enumerates every permutation-like assignment.
feedback.details: This is another example of pruning effectiveness being input-dependent.

items.prompt: A collect-all pruned solver returns s valid solutions, each of length d. Select a lower bound on output work and output space even if all invalid branches are pruned perfectly.
constraints: Every valid solution must be materialized independently.

feedback.reason: Pruning can remove invalid search work but cannot avoid producing required valid outputs.
feedback.details: Output-sensitive cost remains even under ideal pruning.
